questions.stem: Statement: Students who solve more mock tests score higher in the actual exam. Conclusion I: Solving mocks improves exam performance. II: Students who score high solve more mocks.

questions.stem: Statements: All fruits are sweet. Some sweet are sour. No sour is bitter. Conclusions: I. Some fruits are sour. II. Some sweet are not bitter.

questions.stem: In code, 1 = January, 2 = February ... then 9-10-7-1 = ?

questions.stem: Meaning of 'To cost an arm and a leg':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: safety / should / mines / our / be / in / priority / top
options: Safety in mines should be our top priority
In mines safety our top priority should be
Top priority safety should be in mines
Safety should be our top priority in mines
Safety should be our top priority in mines
questions.solution: Safety should be our top priority in mines

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To let the cat out of the bag':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: workers / the / demanded / conditions / working / better
options: Demanded the workers better working conditions
Working conditions demanded the workers better
The workers demanded better working conditions
The workers demanded better working conditions
Better working conditions the workers demanded
questions.solution: The workers demanded better working conditions

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To give the benefit of the doubt':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: solve / team / the / to / problem / together / worked
options: The team worked together to solve the problem
Worked together the team to solve the problem
Together the team worked to solve the problem
The team worked together to solve the problem
To solve the problem the team together worked
questions.solution: The team worked together to solve the problem

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'Bite the bullet':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: digital / has / way / the / changed / communication / we
options: Communication digital has changed the way we
We communicate way the has changed digital technology
Digital technology has changed the way we communicate
The way we communicate has changed digital technology
Digital technology has changed the way we communicate
questions.solution: Digital technology has changed the way we communicate

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To make no bones about':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: team / successfully / the / completed / project / the
options: Successfully the team completed the project
The team successfully completed the project
The project successfully completed the team
Completed the project the team successfully
The team successfully completed the project
questions.solution: The team successfully completed the project

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'The ball is in your court':
options: It is your turn to act
questions.solution: It is your turn to act

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: renewable / future / energy / is / the

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To spill the beans':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: project / the / completed / was / time / on
options: The project was completed on time
Was the project completed on time
Completed was the project on time
The project was completed on time
On time the project was completed
questions.solution: The project was completed on time

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To read between the lines':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: climate / urgent / change / requires / action / addressing
options: Addressing climate change requires urgent action
Climate change addressing urgent action requires
Addressing climate change requires urgent action
Requires urgent action change addressing climate
Urgent action climate change requires addressing
questions.solution: Addressing climate change requires urgent action

questions.stem: Meaning of 'To take with a grain of salt':

questions.stem: Antonym of TENACIOUS:

questions.stem: Synonym of FORTIFY:

questions.stem: He is ____ to the needs of the poor.

questions.stem: Synonym of INNOCUOUS:

questions.stem: The workers went on strike, ____ production.

questions.stem: Antonym of PRUDENT:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Flow in a circular pipe is generally laminar when the Reynolds number is below about:
questions.solution: Below Re ≈ 2000 pipe flow is laminar; above ~4000 it is turbulent.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Brinell and Rockwell tests are used to measure a material's:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A driver gear (30 teeth) at 900 rpm meshes with a 60-tooth gear. The driven gear speed is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A throttling (Joule–Thomson) process across a valve is characterised by constant:
questions.solution: Adiabatic throttling with negligible KE/PE keeps h₁ = h₂; temperature usually changes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An Otto-cycle engine has a compression ratio of 8 (γ = 1.4). Its air-standard efficiency is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Newton's law of cooling describes heat transfer by:
questions.solution: q = hAΔT relates convective heat transfer to the surface–fluid temperature difference.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A four-stroke engine completes one power stroke every:
questions.solution: Suction, compression, power and exhaust occupy two crankshaft revolutions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 10 kN, span L = 8 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 10×8/4 = 20 kN·m.

figure.caption: Simply supported beam, central load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Adding a fin to a surface improves heat dissipation only when the fin's:
questions.solution: A fin helps only if it transfers more heat than the bare base area it covers (effectiveness > 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pin-ended column (E = 200 GPa, I = 16×10⁻⁶ m⁴, length 4 m) has an Euler buckling load of about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Factor of safety is defined as:
questions.solution: FoS = failure stress/allowable working stress; it is always greater than 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ideal-gas equation of state is:
questions.solution: pV = mRT relates pressure, volume and absolute temperature for an ideal gas.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A solid shaft of 30 mm diameter transmits a torque of 600 N·m. The maximum shear stress is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The coefficient of performance (COP) of a refrigerator is:
questions.solution: COP_ref = Q_absorbed/W_input, and can be greater than 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A refrigerator extracts 300 W of heat using 100 W of work. Its COP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A short, stocky member under axial compression most likely fails by ____, while a long slender one fails by ____:
questions.solution: Stocky columns crush at the yield stress; slender columns buckle elastically first.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Clausius inequality ∮ dQ/T ≤ 0 is satisfied with the equality sign only for:
questions.solution: ∮dQ/T = 0 for reversible cycles and < 0 for irreversible ones.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A reversed Carnot refrigerator works between 310 K and 270 K. Its COP is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Slow, continuous deformation of a material under constant load at high temperature is called:
questions.solution: Creep is time-dependent plastic strain that becomes significant at high temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 2000 mm bar elongates by 2 mm under load. Its strain (×10⁻³) is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an adiabatic process the quantity that is zero is:
questions.solution: Adiabatic ⇒ Q = 0; the gas may still do work and change temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pascal's law states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted:
questions.solution: Pressure in a confined static fluid acts equally in every direction — basis of hydraulics.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 300 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

figure.caption: Tensile stress–strain curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SI unit of thermal conductivity is:
questions.solution: Thermal conductivity k is measured in watt per metre per kelvin.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An axial load of 50 kN acts on a bar of 100 mm² cross-section. The normal stress is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle with compression ratio r is:
questions.solution: η_Otto = 1 − 1/r^(γ−1); efficiency rises with compression ratio.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For most engineering metals, the theoretical upper limit of Poisson's ratio is:
questions.solution: ν = 0.5 corresponds to an incompressible material; real metals are ≈ 0.25–0.35.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 12 cm (V₁ = 4 m/s) to 5 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=12cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=5cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=4m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a constant-volume (isochoric) process the work done by a closed system is:
questions.solution: W = ∫p dV = 0 when volume is constant, so all heat changes internal energy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a cycle, 1000 kJ of heat is added and 400 kJ rejected per cycle. The thermal efficiency is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of specific heats is correct for a gas?
questions.solution: At constant pressure some heat does expansion work, so Cp > Cv (Cp − Cv = R for an ideal gas).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Boundary-layer separation on a body occurs primarily because of:
questions.solution: When pressure rises in the flow direction, near-wall fluid decelerates and reverses, separating the layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A specimen shows a stress of 100 MPa at a strain of 0.0005. Its Young's modulus is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The endurance (fatigue) limit of a material is the stress below which it:
options: Can endure virtually infinite load cycles
questions.solution: Below the endurance limit, ferrous materials survive an effectively unlimited number of cycles.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows from a 30 cm² pipe (velocity 2 m/s) into a 5 cm² pipe. The new velocity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The bending stress in a beam varies with distance y from the neutral axis as:
questions.solution: Flexure formula σ = My/I; stress is maximum at the outermost fibre.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Stress concentration at a notch is most severe when the notch is:
questions.solution: The stress-concentration factor rises sharply as the fillet radius decreases.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Carnot engine takes 1500 kJ from a source at 800 K and rejects to 400 K. The work output is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a heat exchanger, LMTD stands for the log-mean of the:
options: Temperature differences at the two ends
questions.solution: LMTD averages the end temperature differences for the heat-transfer rate Q = UA·LMTD.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 20 cm (V₁ = 3 m/s) to 10 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=20cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=10cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=3m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The zeroth law of thermodynamics defines the concept of:
questions.solution: If two bodies are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, they are with each other — the basis of temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The second law of thermodynamics implies that heat, on its own, flows from:
options: A hotter body to a colder body
A colder body to a hotter body
questions.solution: Spontaneous heat flow is from high to low temperature; the reverse needs work (Clausius statement).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A reversed Carnot refrigerator works between 300 K and 260 K. Its COP is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A venturimeter is primarily used to measure:
questions.solution: It uses the pressure drop at a throat (Bernoulli) to find discharge.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A spring of stiffness 500 N/m is stretched 0.1 m. The restoring force is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard cycle for a spark-ignition (petrol) engine is the:
questions.solution: SI engines approximate the constant-volume heat-addition Otto cycle.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Brayton cycle is the ideal cycle for a:
questions.solution: The Brayton (Joule) cycle models gas-turbine plants with constant-pressure heat addition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the p–V diagram shown, two expansions start from the same state (V₁ ≈ 2). The steeper curve (1) is the:
questions.solution: Adiabatic curves (pV^γ) are steeper than isothermal curves (pV) on a p–V diagram because γ > 1.

figure.caption: p–V diagram: adiabatic vs isothermal

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about pipe flow is INCORRECT?
options: Doubling the mean velocity halves the frictional head loss in turbulent flow
Laminar flow has a parabolic velocity profile
Turbulent head loss roughly varies with velocity squared
questions.solution: In turbulent flow head loss ∝ V², so doubling V roughly quadruples it — the statement is wrong.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows at 2 m/s through a pipe of area 0.01 m². The volume flow rate is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If the depth of a rectangular beam is doubled (same width and load), its bending strength (resisting moment) increases by a factor of:
questions.solution: Section modulus Z = bd²/6 ∝ d², so the moment capacity rises 2² = 4×.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Young's modulus is the ratio of:
questions.solution: E = σ/ε for axial loading within the elastic limit.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 10 kN, length L = 3 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 10×3 = 30 kN·m at the fixed end.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ratio of specific heats γ for a gas equals:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 30 kN, span L = 4 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 30×4/4 = 30 kN·m.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Enthalpy h is defined as:
questions.solution: Enthalpy h = u + pv, a convenient property for flow processes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a lathe, the principal cutting operation produces a surface of revolution by:
questions.solution: Turning removes material from a rotating workpiece using a single-point tool.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Carnot engine works between 600 K and 350 K. Its efficiency is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The first law of thermodynamics is essentially a statement of the conservation of:
questions.solution: ΔU = Q − W; energy is conserved as it changes between heat and work.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An axial load of 10 kN acts on a bar of 400 mm² cross-section. The normal stress is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Poisson's ratio is the ratio of:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Hooke's law states that, within the elastic limit, stress is:
questions.solution: σ = Eε within the elastic range; E is Young's modulus.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water issues from an orifice under a head of 10 m. The ideal jet velocity (Torricelli) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fatigue failure of a component typically occurs at a stress that is:
options: Above the ultimate stress in one cycle
Equal to the yield stress every time
Below the static yield stress, after many cycles
questions.solution: Repeated/fluctuating loads cause crack growth and failure below the static yield strength.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the p–V diagram shown, two expansions start from the same state (V₁ ≈ 1). The steeper curve (1) is the:
questions.solution: Adiabatic curves (pV^γ) are steeper than isothermal curves (pV) on a p–V diagram because γ > 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Rankine cycle is the ideal cycle for a:
questions.solution: The Rankine cycle (boiler–turbine–condenser–pump) models vapour power plants.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the effectiveness–NTU method, NTU (number of transfer units) is defined as:
questions.solution: NTU = UA/C_min characterises the heat-exchanger size relative to the minimum heat-capacity rate.

questions.stem: A shaft transmits a torque of 150 N·m at 600 rpm. The power transmitted is about:

questions.stem: In an isothermal process the property held constant is:

questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 10 kN, length L = 2 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 10×2 = 20 kN·m at the fixed end.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pitot tube measures the:
questions.solution: It senses stagnation pressure; the difference from static pressure gives velocity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard cycle for a compression-ignition (diesel) engine is the:
questions.solution: CI engines approximate the constant-pressure heat-addition Diesel cycle.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A wall (k = 50 W/m·K, area 0.5 m², thickness 0.2 m) has a 100 K temperature difference. The conduction rate is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gyroscopic couple acting on a rotating disc of moment of inertia I, spin ω and precession Ω is:
questions.solution: Gyroscopic couple C = Iω·Ω, perpendicular to both spin and precession axes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gauge pressure at a depth of 10 m in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The COP of a reversed Carnot refrigerator working between T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold) is:
questions.solution: COP_ref = T_cold/(T_hot − T_cold), with absolute temperatures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gauge pressure at depth h in a liquid of density ρ is:
questions.solution: Hydrostatic pressure p = ρgh increases linearly with depth.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water issues from an orifice under a head of 45 m. The ideal jet velocity (Torricelli) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For any irreversible process in an isolated system, the total entropy:
questions.solution: The second law: entropy of an isolated system increases for irreversible processes (= 0 only if reversible).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A heat exchanger has end temperature differences of 80 K and 40 K. The LMTD is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For a circular shaft in torsion, the relation T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L is the:
questions.solution: The torsion equation links torque, polar moment J, shear stress and twist.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A sudden change of cross-section in a loaded part causes:
questions.solution: Geometric discontinuities raise local stress well above the nominal value.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water (ν = 10⁻⁶ m²/s) flows at 1 m/s in a 0.1 m pipe. The Reynolds number is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For steady incompressible flow in a pipe, the continuity equation gives:
questions.solution: Mass conservation for constant density: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A shaft transmits a torque of 50 N·m at 1200 rpm. The power transmitted is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The heat-treatment process used to soften steel and relieve internal stresses is:
questions.solution: Annealing heats and slowly cools steel, softening it and relieving stresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bernoulli's equation is fundamentally a statement of:
options: Conservation of energy along a streamline
questions.solution: It balances pressure, kinetic and potential energy per unit weight along a streamline (no losses).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A refrigerator extracts 200 W of heat using 150 W of work. Its COP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Reynolds number represents the ratio of:
questions.solution: Re = ρVD/μ = inertia/viscous; it indicates laminar vs turbulent flow.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 12 cm (V₁ = 2 m/s) to 5 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Between two fixed temperature reservoirs, the Carnot engine is special because it has:
options: The maximum work but lower efficiency than real engines
The maximum possible efficiency, being fully reversible
questions.solution: No engine between the same two temperatures can exceed Carnot efficiency, because Carnot is reversible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about a refrigerator's COP is correct?
options: It equals the Carnot engine efficiency
questions.solution: COP_ref = Q_cold/W can be well above 1 because it moves more heat than the work supplied.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 250 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold), in kelvin, is:
questions.solution: Carnot efficiency η = 1 − T₂/T₁ with temperatures in kelvin.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 350 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: During the melting of ice at atmospheric pressure, while heat is being added the temperature:
questions.solution: Latent heat of fusion changes phase at constant temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Rank the typical thermal conductivities from highest to lowest:
questions.solution: Free electrons make metals best conductors; gases, with sparse molecules, are poorest.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 10 kg body moves at 6 m/s. Its kinetic energy is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Stefan–Boltzmann law states that radiated energy is proportional to:
options: The fourth power of absolute temperature
questions.solution: E = σT⁴ for a black body; radiation rises steeply with temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A cantilever (50×100 mm section) of length 1 m carries an end load of 2 kN. The maximum bending stress is about:
